title: EXPOSÉ X EULER-POINCARÉ FORMULA IN ÉTALE COHOMOLOGY

pages.title: EXPOSÉ X EULER-POINCARÉ FORMULA IN ÉTALE COHOMOLOGY

blocks.html:
EXPOSÉ X
EULER-POINCARÉ FORMULA IN ÉTALE COHOMOLOGYby A. GROTHENDIECK
(Written up by I. BUCUR)

The aim of the present exposé is the statement and proof of formula (7.2), of Euler-Poincaré type. This formula generalizes a formula obtained independently by Ogg and Chafarevitch, whose results are presented (from the point of view of étale cohomology) in the exposé of M. Raynaud [1].
The Euler-Poincaré formula makes essential use of the formalism of derived categories. As in loc.cit. the proof proceeds via a variant (5.1) of a well-known formula of Weil.

pages.title: 1. Sheaves on a scheme with operators.

blocks.html: 1. Sheaves on a scheme with operators.

Let \(X\) be a scheme equipped with a group \(G\) operating by automorphisms. Proceeding as in ([T] chap. V) one can define the notion of an étale sheaf on \(X\) with group of operators \(G\), or simply "sheaf on \((X_{\mathrm{et}},G)\)".
By definition, to give an étale sheaf on \(X\) with group of operators \(G\) is to give a sheaf \(F\in\operatorname{Ob}(\widetilde X_{\mathrm{et}})\) and a morphism
\[S_g:F\longrightarrow g^*(F)\]
for each \(g\in G\), in such a way that the following conditions are fulfilled:
a) \(S_e=1_F\)
b) For \(g,h\in G\), the diagram:
is commutative, where \(C_{h,g}(F):(hg)^*(F)\longrightarrow(h^*g^*)(F)\) is the canonical isomorphism.

Morphisms between two sheaves \((F,S_g)\), \((F',S'_g)\) on \(X\) with group of operators \(G\) are defined in an obvious way, and we obtain a category \(\widetilde X_{\mathrm{et},G}\) which is a topos, as follows easily from Giraud's criterion (SGA 4 II 4.12).
A ring \(A\) of \(\widetilde X_{\mathrm{et},G}\) being given, we can consider the abelian category of \(A\)-Modules, which will be denoted by \(\operatorname{Mod}(A)\).

For example, a ring \(\Lambda\) being given, we can trivially endow the constant sheaf \(\Lambda_X\) associated with \(\Lambda\) with a structure of sheaf with group of operators \(G\), by using the canonical transitivity isomorphisms
\[S_g:\Lambda_X\xrightarrow{\sim}g^*(\Lambda_X).\]
All the results proved in [T] chap. V can be extended to this setting, and we leave the details to the reader.

pages.title: 2. In which we prove that a certain complex of \(\Lambda[G]\)-modules is perfect

blocks.html: 2. In which we prove that a certain complex of \(\Lambda[G]\)-modules is perfect
Let us recall the following result from (SGA 4 XVII):

blocks.title: Lemma 2.1.
blocks.html:
Lemma 2.1.

Let \(X\) and \(Y\) be two schemes and \(f:X\longrightarrow Y\) a proper morphism, with \(Y\) quasi-compact. Let us denote by \(A\) a torsion ring, and let \(F^\bullet\) be a complex of sheaves of \(A\)-modules on \(X\) of finite tor-dimension (SGA 4 XVII) ; then \(Rf_*(F^\bullet)\) is of finite tor-dimension.

blocks.title: Proposition 2.2.
blocks.html:
Proposition 2.2.

Let \(X\) be a scheme proper over the separably closed field \(k\), \(G\) a finite group operating on \(X\), \(V\) an open subset of \(X\) stable under \(G\) and such that \(G\) operates admissibly (SGA 1 V 1.7) and freely on \(V\), \(\Lambda=\mathbb Z/\ell^n\mathbb Z\) with \(\ell\) a prime number different from the characteristic of \(k\), and \(\Lambda_V\) the constant sheaf on \(V\) associated with \(\Lambda\), equipped with its trivial structure of sheaf with group of operators \(G\). Let us denote by \(i:V\longrightarrow X\) the immersion of \(V\) into \(X\) and let \(\Lambda_{V,X}=i_!(\Lambda_V)\). Under these conditions the complex of \(\Lambda[G]\)-modules \(R\Gamma_X(\Lambda_{V,X})\) is a perfect complex (VIII 7).
Proof: If \(p:X\longrightarrow Y=X/G\) is the canonical projection, it is well known that we have the canonical isomorphism
\[R\Gamma_X(\Lambda_{V,X})\simeq R\Gamma_Y(Rp_*(\Lambda_{V,X}))\]

in the category \(D(\Lambda[G])\). On the other hand the complex \(Rp_*(\Lambda_{V,X})\) of \(\Lambda[G]\)-modules on \(Y\) is of finite tor-dimension. Indeed, \(p:X\longrightarrow Y\) being a finite morphism of schemes, we have \(R^qp_*(\Lambda_{V,X})=0\) if \(q\ne0\) (SGA 4 VIII 5.5). It therefore follows that:
\[Rp_*(\Lambda_{V,X})=p_*(\Lambda_{V,X})\]
(SGA 4 VIII 5.5) also gives us the possibility of determining the stalks of the sheaf \(p_*(\Lambda_{V,X})\), and we find, if we take into account the fact that \(G\) operates freely on \(V\):
\[ p_*(\Lambda_{V,X})_{\bar y}\simeq \begin{cases} \displaystyle\prod_{x\in p^{-1}(y)}\Lambda_{V,X,x}\simeq\Lambda[G]&\text{if }y\in p(V),\\[6pt] 0&\text{if }y\notin p(V) \end{cases} \]
(\(\bar y\) is the geometric point of \(Y\) localized at \(y\)).

Consequently \(Rp_*(\Lambda_{V,X})=p_*(\Lambda_{V,X})\) is flat over \(\Lambda[G]\), being so stalk by stalk (SGA 4 XVII), hence of finite tor-dimension. Applying 2.1 to the canonical morphism \(Y\longrightarrow\operatorname{Spec}(k)\) we deduce that
\[R\Gamma_X(\Lambda_{V,X})=R\Gamma_Y(Rp_*(\Lambda_{V,X}))\]
is of finite tor-dimension.

To conclude that \(R\Gamma_X(\Lambda_{V,X})\) is perfect it remains for us to prove that it is pseudo-coherent and with bounded cohomology (VIII 8.1). But \(\Lambda[G]\) being noetherian, to verify that \(R\Gamma_X(\Lambda_{V,X})\) is pseudo-coherent it suffices to show that the \(H^q(X,\Lambda_{V,X})\) are of finite type over \(\Lambda[G]\) (VIII 7.2), or what amounts to the same thing, over \(\Lambda\), which follows from (SGA 4 XIV 1.1) applied to the \(\Lambda\)-Module \(\Lambda_{V,X}\).
The Weil formula 6.1 will give an expression for the class
\[\operatorname{cl}_{K^\bullet(\Lambda[G])}\bigl(R\Gamma_X(\Lambda_{V,X})\bigr),\]
for the case of a curve \(X\), in terms of known global invariants and of local invariants which we shall define in n° 5.

pages.title: 3. Reminders on linear representations of finite groups.

blocks.html: 3. Reminders on linear representations of finite groups.
In this n°, \(\Lambda\) denotes a commutative ring, and \(G\) a group.

Let us consider the full subcategory \(\mathcal C\) of \(\operatorname{Mod}(\Lambda[G])\) of \(\Lambda[G]\)-modules \(M\) whose underlying \(\Lambda\)-module is projective of finite type. For such a module \(M\) and for a \(g\in G\) we consider the endomorphism (for the \(\Lambda\)-module structure)
\[g_M:M\longrightarrow M,\qquad g_M(x)=gx.\]
We obtain a function
\[\chi_M:G\longrightarrow\Lambda,\qquad \chi_M(g)=\operatorname{Tr}_M(g),\]
which is a central function on \(G\) and which is called the character of the \(\Lambda[G]\)-module \(M\).

If we associate with each \(M\in\operatorname{Ob}(\mathcal C)\) its character \(\chi_M\) we obtain an additive function (VIII 1). Using the sorite of traces for endomorphisms of perfect complexes, we even obtain an additive function \(\operatorname{Tr}\) on the category of complexes of \(\Lambda[G]\)-modules which are perfect as complexes of \(\Lambda\)-modules. Whence a homomorphism
\[R^\bullet_\Lambda(G)\xrightarrow{\operatorname{Tr}}\mathcal F(G,\Lambda),\]
where \(R^\bullet_\Lambda(G)\) is the representation ring of \(G\) over \(\Lambda\) (VIII 10), \(\mathcal F(G,\Lambda)\) the module of central functions \(G\longrightarrow\Lambda\).
When \(G\) is finite, the composite of the canonical homomorphism
\[K^\bullet(\Lambda[G])\longrightarrow R^\bullet_\Lambda(G)\]
with \(\operatorname{Tr}\) will be denoted by \(\operatorname{tr}\).
Let us recall the following result (IX th.1, cor. 3):

blocks.title: Proposition
blocks.html:
Proposition 3.2. — Let \(\mathbb Z_\ell\) be the ring of \(\ell\)-adic integers and \(G\) a finite group. Then the homomorphism
\[\operatorname{tr}:K^\bullet(\mathbb Z_\ell[G])\longrightarrow\mathcal F(G,\mathbb Z_\ell)\]

is a monomorphism. Two projective \(\mathbb Z_\ell[G]\)-modules having the same trace function in \(\mathcal F(G,\mathbb Z_\ell)\) are isomorphic.
Let \(\alpha:G\longrightarrow G'\) be a homomorphism from a group \(G\) into \(G'\). By restriction of operators we obtain an exact functor:
\[J:\operatorname{Mod}(\Lambda[G'])\longrightarrow\operatorname{Mod}(\Lambda[G]).\]
This functor admits a left adjoint:
\[I:\operatorname{Mod}(\Lambda[G])\longrightarrow\operatorname{Mod}(\Lambda[G']),\]
namely \(I(F)=\Lambda[G']\otimes_{\Lambda[G]}F\). If \(F\) is projective of finite type (resp. of finite type) then so is \(I(F)\).
If \(\varphi\in\mathcal F(G',\Lambda)\) is a central function on \(G'\), the function \(\varphi\circ\alpha\) is a central function on \(G\) and we consequently obtain the map
\[\alpha_*:\mathcal F(G',\Lambda)\longrightarrow\mathcal F(G,\Lambda).\]
If \(\varphi\) is the character of a \(\Lambda[G']\)-module \(M\) in \(C(G')\), \(\alpha_*(\varphi)\) is the character of the \(\Lambda[G]\)-module \(J(M)\).

Suppose now that \(\alpha:G\longrightarrow G'\) is an injection and that \(G\) is of finite index in \(G'\). In this case if \(F\) is a \(\Lambda[G]\)-module in \(C(G)\) whose character is \(\psi:G\longrightarrow\Lambda\), then \(I(F)\) is in \(C(G')\) and the character \(\psi^*:G'\longrightarrow\Lambda\) of \(I(F)\) is the character "induced" by \(\psi\), i.e. the function on \(G'\) deduced from \(\psi\) by the formula:
with the convention that \(\psi(t^{-1}gt)=0\) if \(t^{-1}gt\notin G\).

Let \(X^\bullet\) and \(Y^\bullet\) be two complexes of \(A\)-modules. Just as in the case of modules, if one of the complexes \(X^\bullet\), \(Y^\bullet\) has an additional structure of \(A\)-\(B\)-bimodule, then it is possible to introduce on \(\operatorname{Hom}_A^\bullet(X^\bullet,Y^\bullet)\) a structure of complex of \(B\)-modules and consequently a corresponding structure on \(R\operatorname{Hom}_A(X^\bullet,Y^\bullet)\).

Suppose for example that \(X^\bullet\) is an object of \(D^-(A)\) and \(Y^\bullet\) is a complex of \(A\)-\(B\)-bimodules, say to be precise that we are in the case described by the symbol \({}_A{}_B Y^\bullet\). In particular \(Y^\bullet\) is an object of \(D(A)\). Let \(P^\bullet\longrightarrow X^\bullet\) be a projective resolution of \(X^\bullet\). Then \(\operatorname{Hom}_A^\bullet(P^\bullet,Y^\bullet)\) is obviously a complex of left \(B\)-modules, that is to say an object of \(D(B)\). If we use another projective resolution \(P_1^\bullet\longrightarrow X^\bullet\), the complex of \(B\)-modules \(\operatorname{Hom}_A^\bullet(P_1^\bullet,Y^\bullet)\) is canonically isomorphic in \(D(B)\) to the complex \(\operatorname{Hom}_A^\bullet(P^\bullet,Y^\bullet)\). It therefore follows that we can define \(R\operatorname{Hom}_A(X^\bullet,Y^\bullet)\) as an object of \(D(B)\) determined up to unique isomorphism, depending functorially on \(X^\bullet\) in \(D^-(A)\) and \(Y^\bullet\) in \(D(A\otimes_{\mathbb Z}B)\).

Analogous considerations apply to the case of the complex \(X^\bullet\mathbin{\overset L\otimes}_A Y^\bullet\). If for example \(Y^\bullet\) is a complex of \(A\)-\(B\)-bimodules as above and \(X\) is a complex of right \(A\)-modules bounded on the right, we can define \(X^\bullet\mathbin{\overset L\otimes}_A Y^\bullet\) as an object of \(D(B)\) determined up to unique isomorphism.
Let \(\Lambda\) be a commutative ring, \(A\) and \(B\) two algebras over \(\Lambda\) and
\[C=A\otimes_\Lambda B.\]
The \(C\)-modules are thus the \(A\)-\(B\)-bimodules.

If \(\Delta\) is a full triangulated subcategory of \(D(A)\), we shall denote by \(D(C)_\Delta\) the full triangulated subcategory of \(D(C)\) generated by the complexes which, considered as objects of \(D(A)\), belong to \(\Delta\).

blocks.title: Proposition
blocks.html:
Proposition 3.6. — The notations being those of 3.4 and 3.5, let \(\Delta\) be a full triangulated subcategory of \(D(A)\) stable under direct factors. If \(P^\bullet\) is an object of \(D(B)_{\mathrm{parf}}\) (VIII 7) and \(M\) is an object of \(D(C)_\Delta\), the complex of \(A\)-modules \(R\operatorname{Hom}_B(P^\bullet,M^\bullet)\in D(A)\) defined in 4.1 is an object of \(\Delta\). In particular there results a pairing of triangulated categories
\[(3.6.1)\qquad R\operatorname{Hom}:D(B)_{\mathrm{parf}}\times D(C)_\Delta\longrightarrow\Delta,\]

that is to say a bifunctor exact in each variable, and consequently (VIII 3) a bilinear map:

\[(3.6.2)\qquad K^\bullet(B)\times K^\bullet(D(C)_\Delta)\xrightarrow{\ \mathrm{hom}\ }K(\Delta)\]

such that

\[(3.6.3)\qquad \operatorname{hom}(\operatorname{cl}_{K^\bullet(B)}(P^\bullet),\operatorname{cl}_{K^\bullet(D(C)_\Delta)}(M^\bullet))=\operatorname{cl}_{K(\Delta)}(R\operatorname{Hom}_B(P^\bullet,M^\bullet)).\]

We obtain an analogous statement if we replace \(B\) by its opposite \(B^\circ\) and \(R\operatorname{Hom}_B(P,M)\) by \(P^\bullet\mathbin{\overset L\otimes}_B M^\bullet\).

Proof. We first show that \(R\operatorname{Hom}_B(P,M)\) is an object of \(\Delta\) in the particular case where \(P^\bullet\) has a single non-zero component, then we argue by induction on the number of non-zero components of \(P^\bullet\).

Let \(P\) be a \(\Lambda[G]\)-module. It is in particular a \(\Lambda\)-module. We can endow the \(\Lambda\)-module \(\check P=\operatorname{Hom}_\Lambda(P,\Lambda)\) with a structure of left \(\Lambda[G]\)-module, by setting for each \(u\in\operatorname{Hom}_\Lambda(P,\Lambda)\) and \(g\in G\), \((gu)(x)=u(g^{-1}x)\), i.e.:
The module \(\check P\) over \(\Lambda[G]\) thus obtained will be called the dual of \(P\).
We can thus define the dual \(\check P^\bullet=\operatorname{Hom}_\Lambda^\bullet(P^\bullet,\Lambda)\) of a complex \(P\) of \(\Lambda[G]\)-modules as being a complex of \(\Lambda[G]\)-modules.
If \(\chi_P\) is the character of \(P\), the character \(\chi_{\check P}\) of its dual is given by the formula:
which is deduced immediately from (4.3.1) by using the formula:
\[\operatorname{Tr}_\Lambda^*(v)=\operatorname{Tr}_\Lambda^*({}^tv).\]

If \(P\) is a projective \(\Lambda[G]\)-module of finite type it is clear that \(\check P\) is also a projective \(\Lambda[G]\)-module of finite type. Moreover, in this case, the canonical homomorphism of \(\Lambda\)-modules:
\[P\longrightarrow\check{\check P}\]
is in fact an isomorphism of \(\Lambda[G]\)-modules. In this way we define on \(K^\bullet(\Lambda[G])\) an involution
\[x\longmapsto\check x,\]

which associates with the class of \(P\) the class of \(\check P\). It is immediate that if \(\mu:\Lambda\longrightarrow\Gamma\) is a homomorphism of commutative rings and \(u:\Lambda[G]\longrightarrow\Gamma[G]\) the associated homomorphism, the homomorphism \(u^*:K^\bullet(\Lambda[G])\longrightarrow K^\bullet(\Gamma[G])\) commutes with the involutions \(x\longmapsto\check x\) on
\(K^\bullet(\Lambda[G])\) and \(K^\bullet(\Gamma[G])\).
Let \(A\) be an algebra over \(\Lambda\) and
\[A[G]=\Lambda[G]\otimes_\Lambda A.\]

A complex \(X^\bullet\) of \(A[G]\)-modules being given, we denote by \(X^{\bullet G}\) the complex of \(A\)-modules which is the image of \(X^\bullet\) under the functor which associates with each \(A[G]\)-module \(X\) the module over \(A\) formed by the elements of \(X\) invariant under \(G\).

blocks.title: Proposition
blocks.html:
Proposition 3.8. — Let \(G\) be a finite group, \(P^\bullet\) a bounded complex of \(\Lambda[G]\)-modules all of whose components are projective of finite type, and \(M\) a complex of \(A[G]\)-modules. Under these conditions there exist canonical isomorphisms of complexes of \(A\)-modules:
\[(3.8.1)\qquad \operatorname{Hom}_{\Lambda[G]}^\bullet(P^\bullet,M)\simeq\operatorname{Hom}_\Lambda^\bullet(P^\bullet,M)^G\simeq(\check P^\bullet\otimes_\Lambda M^\bullet)^G\simeq\check P^\bullet\otimes_{\Lambda[G]}M^\bullet.\]

The proof uses the well-known isomorphisms for the case of modules, and is left to the reader. Note that in the second and third terms, the operations of \(G\) on \(\operatorname{Hom}_\Lambda^\bullet(P^\bullet,M^\bullet)\) resp. \(\check P^\bullet\otimes_\Lambda M^\bullet\) are defined by transport of structure from its operations on \(P^\bullet\) and \(M^\bullet\) resp. \(\check P^\bullet\) and \(\check M^\bullet\).

blocks.title: Corollary
blocks.html:
Corollary 3.9. — If \(P^\bullet\in\operatorname{Ob}(D(\Lambda[G])_{\mathrm{parf}})\) and \(M^\bullet\in D^+(A[G])\) then we have in the derived category \(D(A)\) the following isomorphisms:
\[(3.9.1)\qquad R\operatorname{Hom}_{\Lambda[G]}(P^\bullet,M^\bullet)=R\Gamma^G(\check P^\bullet\mathbin{\overset L\otimes}_\Lambda M)\simeq\check P^\bullet\mathbin{\overset L\otimes}_{\Lambda[G]}M^\bullet.\]
Indeed, thanks to the hypothesis \(P^\bullet\in D(\Lambda[G])_{\mathrm{parf}}\), we may assume that \(P^\bullet\) is bounded and all its components are projective of finite
type. We thus have
\[R\operatorname{Hom}_{\Lambda[G]}(P^\bullet,M^\bullet)=\operatorname{Hom}_{\Lambda[G]}^\bullet(P^\bullet,M^\bullet)\]
and we can use the isomorphisms (4.3.4). But \(\check P^\bullet\) is, like \(P^\bullet\), bounded with projective components of finite type, hence
\[\check P^\bullet\mathbin{\overset L\otimes}_{\Lambda[G]}M^\bullet=\check P^\bullet\otimes_{\Lambda[G]}M^\bullet.\]

Similarly \(\check P^\bullet\mathbin{\overset L\otimes}_\Lambda M^\bullet=\check P^\bullet\otimes_\Lambda M^\bullet\). Moreover the components of the complex \(\check P^\bullet\otimes_\Lambda M^\bullet\) are cohomologically trivial ([3] IX 3) and we thus have:
\[R\Gamma^G(\check P^\bullet\mathbin{\overset L\otimes}_\Lambda M^\bullet)=(\check P^\bullet\otimes_\Lambda M^\bullet)^G.\]
4. The Swan representation.

Let \(E\) be a smooth connected algebraic curve over the algebraically closed field \(k\). If \(x\in E\) is a closed point of \(E\), its local ring \(\mathcal O_{E,x}\) (or simply \(\mathcal O_x\)) is a discrete valuation ring whose field of fractions is isomorphic to the field of rational functions on \(E\) and whose residue field is \(k\). The corresponding valuation will be denoted by \(v_x\).

blocks.title: Lemma 4.1.
blocks.html:
Lemma 4.1.

Let \(E\) be a smooth algebraic curve over the algebraically closed field \(k\) and \(g:E\longrightarrow E\) an endomorphism of \(E\) different from the identity. Suppose that \(x\in E\) is an isolated fixed point of \(g\) and let \(\pi\) be a uniformizer of \(\mathcal O_{E,x}\). If \(v_x\) is the corresponding valuation of \(\mathcal O_{E,x}\), then the multiplicity \(v_x(g)\) of the fixed point \(x\) of the map \(g\) (IV) is equal to \(v_x(g(\pi)-\pi)\).
In the product \(E\times E\) the diagonal and the graph of the endomorphism \(g\) will be denoted respectively by \(\Delta\) and \(\Gamma_g\). Let
\[\gamma:E\longrightarrow E\times E,\qquad \delta:E\longrightarrow E\times E\]
be the respective immersions. They are given, on the completed local rings of \(x\) resp. \((x,x)\), by the homomorphisms
\[\gamma^*,\delta^*:A\widehat\otimes A\longrightarrow A\qquad(A=\widehat{\mathcal O}_x),\]
where
\[\gamma^*(a\otimes b)=ab,\qquad \delta^*(a\otimes b)=a g^*(b).\]

It is a matter of evaluating the multiplicity \(v_x(g)\) of the point \(\delta(x)\) in the intersection \(\Delta\Gamma_g\) on \(E\times E\). Now the divisor \(\Delta\) corresponds to the ideal generated by the element \(1\otimes\pi-\pi\otimes1\) of \(A\widehat\otimes A\). Hence the multiplicity of \(\delta(x)\) in the intersection \(\Delta\Gamma_g\) is
\[v_x(g)=v_x\bigl(\delta^*(1\otimes\pi-\pi\otimes1)\bigr)=v_x(g(\pi)-\pi),\qquad\text{q.e.d.}\]

Let \(C\) be a smooth, proper and connected algebraic curve over the algebraically closed field \(k\). If \(\eta\) is its generic point, let us denote by \(K=k(\eta)\) its field of rational functions.
Let
\[K'\supset K,\qquad [K':K]=n\]
be a finite Galois extension of degree \(n\) and with Galois group \(G\). Let us denote by \(C'\) the normalization of \(C\) in \(K'\) and let
\[p:C'\longrightarrow C\]
be the canonical projection. The Galois group \(G\) obviously operates on \(C'\). The stabilizer of \(y'\in C'\) in \(G\) will be denoted \(G_{y'}\).
If \(\pi\) is a uniformizer of \(\mathcal O_{C',y'}\) and if \(s\in G_{y'}\), \(s\ne e\), let us set
(the multiplicity of the fixed point \(y'\) for the map \(s:C'\longrightarrow C'\) (Lemma 5.1)).
The Swan representation associated with \(y'\) is defined by its character \(\sigma_{y'}\), which is given by the formula:
\[(4.2.2)\qquad \sigma_{y'}(s)= \begin{cases} 1-v_{y'}(s)&\text{if }s\ne e,\\ \displaystyle\sum_{s\ne e}(v_{y'}(s)-1)&\text{if }s=e. \end{cases}\]

The results of Artin-Serre-Swan (IX Th 4) show that, \(\ell\) being a prime number distinct from the characteristic of \(k\), there exists a projective \(\mathbb Z_\ell[G_{y'}]\)-module of finite type \(\operatorname{Sw}_{y'}\), determined up to isomorphism, which has character \(\sigma_{y'}\).
It is also of interest to consider the \(\mathbb Z_\ell[G_{y'}]\)-module
\[(4.2.3)\qquad \operatorname{Sw}'_{y'}=\operatorname{Sw}_{y'}\otimes_{\mathbb Z_\ell}\mathbb Z_\ell[G_{y'}].\]
The character \(\sigma'_{y'}\) of \(\operatorname{Sw}'_{y'}\) is given by the formula:
\[(4.2.4)\qquad \sigma'_{y'}(s)= \begin{cases} 1-v_{y'}(s)&\text{if }s\ne e,\\ \displaystyle 1+\sum_{s\ne e}v_{y'}(s)=1+v_{y'}(\mathfrak D_{C'/C})&\text{if }s=e, \end{cases}\]
where \(\mathfrak D_{C'/C}\) is the different of the covering \(C'/C\), the last formula being none other than [3, Chap. VI 2, prop. 4] where we take \(H=\{1\}\).
Finally, the function

where \(u_{G_{y'}}\) denotes the character of the augmentation ideal of \(\mathbb Z_\ell[G]\), is also the character of a \(\mathbb Q_\ell[G_{y'}]\)-module, the Artin representation of \(G_{y'}\), which will be denoted by \(\operatorname{Art}_{y'}\).
We thus have:
\[(4.2.6)\qquad a_{y'}(s)= \begin{cases} -v_{y'}(s)&\text{if }s\ne e,\\ \displaystyle\sum_{s\ne e}v_{y'}(s)=v_{y'}(\mathfrak D_{C/C'})&\text{if }s=e. \end{cases}\]
Let us now consider the inclusion \(G_{y'}\subset G\). We set:

\[(4.2.7)\qquad \operatorname{Sw}_y=\operatorname{Sw}_{y'}\otimes_{\mathbb Z_\ell[G_{y'}]}\mathbb Z_\ell[G],\qquad \operatorname{Sw}'_y=\operatorname{Sw}'_{y'}\otimes_{\mathbb Z_\ell[G_{y'}]}\mathbb Z_\ell[G],\] \[(4.2.8)\qquad \operatorname{Art}_y=\operatorname{Art}_{y'}\otimes_{\mathbb Q_\ell[G_{y'}]}\mathbb Q_\ell[G].\]
The characters of the preceding representations are denoted \(\sigma_y,\sigma'_y,a_y\).

Let us note that the modules thus obtained depend — up to isomorphism — only on the point \(y\in C\), and not on the choice of the point \(y'\) and \(C'\) above \(y\). So let \(y''\) be another such point. There then exists \(g\in G\) such that \(y''=g\cdot y'\). The situation \((C',y'',G)\) is thus deduced from the situation \((C,y',G)\) by the isomorphism \(g\) on \(C'\), \(\operatorname{int}(g):h\longmapsto ghg^{-1}\) on \(G\). We conclude by transport of structure that the modules \(\operatorname{Sw}_y\) etc. associated with \(y''\) are deduced from those associated with \(y'\) by extension of operators by means of \(\operatorname{int}(g):G\longrightarrow G\). We know that this extension transforms a module into an isomorphic module.

blocks.title: Proposition 4.4.
blocks.html:
Proposition 4.4. — The notations being those of 3.7 with \(\Lambda=\mathbb Z_\ell\) and \(M^\bullet\) being an object of \(D^+(A[G])\), we have isomorphisms:
\[(*)\qquad \operatorname{Sw}_y\simeq\check{\operatorname{Sw}}_y\] \[(**)\qquad \operatorname{Hom}_{\mathbb Z_\ell[G]}^\bullet(\operatorname{Sw}_y,M^\bullet)\simeq\operatorname{Sw}_y\otimes_{\mathbb Z_\ell[G]}M^\bullet.\]

Proof. For two projective \(\mathbb Z_\ell[G]\)-modules of finite type to be isomorphic it suffices that they have the same character (3.2). Now if we denote by \(\check\sigma_y\) the character of \(\check{\operatorname{Sw}}_y\) we have (3.7.2):
\[\check\sigma_y(s)=\sigma_y(s^{-1}).\]
On the other hand we have:
hence \(\operatorname{Sw}_{y'}\simeq\check{\operatorname{Sw}}_{y'}\), whence formula (*) by definition (4.2.7). Formula (**) follows thanks to 3.8.
We shall need below an explicit formula for the character of the Swan representation. We have in fact the formulas:

\[(4.5.1)\qquad \sigma_{y'}^*(s)=\sigma_{y''}^*(s)=\sigma_y(s)= \begin{cases} \displaystyle\sum_{\substack{p(z')=y\\s(z')=z'}}(1-v_{z'}(s))&\text{if }s\ne e,\\[6pt] \displaystyle\sum_{p(z')=y}\bigl(1+v_{z'}(\mathfrak D_{C'/C})\bigr)-n&\text{if }s=e, \end{cases}\] \[(4.5.2)\qquad \sigma_{y'}'{}^*(s)=\sigma_{y''}'{}^*(s)=\sigma'_y(s)= \begin{cases} \displaystyle\sum_{\substack{p(z')=y\\s(z')=z'}}(1-v_{z'}(s))&\text{if }s\ne e,\\[6pt] \displaystyle\sum_{p(z')=y}\bigl(1+v_{z'}(\mathfrak D_{C'/C})\bigr)&\text{if }s=e, \end{cases}\] \[(4.5.3)\qquad a_{y'}^*(s)=a_{y''}^*(s)=a_y(s)= \begin{cases} -\displaystyle\sum_{\substack{p(z')=y\\s(z')=z'}}v_{z'}(s)&\text{if }s\ne e,\\[6pt] \displaystyle\sum_{p(z')=y}v_{z'}(\mathfrak D_{C'/C})&\text{if }s=e. \end{cases}\]

To deduce these formulas we use (3.3.1) and take into account the fact that if we fix a point \(y'_0\in C'\), then we can find, for each \(y'\in C'\) such that \(p(y')=p(y'_0)\), an element \(g'\in G\) such that \(g'(y'_0)=y'\). The elements \(g'\) constitute a system of representatives for the right cosets of \(G_{y'_0}\) in \(G\) and we have moreover \(G_{y'}=g'G_{y'_0}g'^{-1}\). This system of representatives can be used in the second member of formula (3.3.1), and we obtain the formulas written.

The invariants \(\operatorname{Sw}_y\), \(\operatorname{Sw}'_y\), \(\operatorname{Art}_y\) can be defined under more general conditions. For this, let \(C'\) be a smooth algebraic curve over \(k\), not necessarily connected. Suppose that the finite group \(G\) operates on \(C'\) and that there exists an open subset \(U\) of \(C=C'/G\) dense in \(C\) such that \(C'|U\) is a principal covering of \(U\) with group \(G\). For a point \(y\in C\) we can define the modules \(\operatorname{Sw}_y\), \(\operatorname{Sw}'_y\), \(\operatorname{Art}_y\) by proceeding as above. We can reduce to the case where \(C'\) is connected in the following way. Let \(y'\in C'\) be such that \(p(y')=y\), \(C'_1\) the irreducible component of \(C'\) which contains \(y'\) and \(C_1\) the irreducible component of \(C\) which contains \(y\). If \(G_{C'_1}\) is the stabilizer of the component \(C'_1\) and if \(\eta'_1\) (resp. \(\eta_1\)) is the generic point of \(C'_1\) (resp. \(C_1\)) then the extension

is Galois with Galois group \(G_{C'_1}\) and \(C'_1\) is the normalization of \(C_1\) in \(K'_1\) (SGA 1 I 10). We can therefore apply the considerations made above to the curves \(C'_1\), \(C_1\) and the group \(G_{C'_1}\). In this way we obtain the projective \(\mathbb Z_\ell[G_{C'_1}]\)-modules of finite type \(\operatorname{Sw}_{G_{C'_1},y}\), \(\operatorname{Sw}'_{G_{C'_1},y}\), \(\operatorname{Art}_{G_{C'_1},y}\), and \(\operatorname{Sw}_y\), \(\operatorname{Sw}'_y\), \(\operatorname{Art}_y\) are the induced modules associated with the inclusion \(G_{C'_1}\subset G\).

Remark 4.6. The modules \(\operatorname{Sw}_{y'}\), \(\operatorname{Sw}'_{y'}\), \(\operatorname{Art}_{y'}\) are purely local invariants: they are known from the group \(G_{y'}\) operating on the completion of the local ring \(\mathcal O_{C',y'}\). Moreover we have \(\operatorname{Sw}_{y'}=0\) if and only if \(p:C'\longrightarrow C\) is tamely ramified at \(y'\) or, what amounts to the same thing, if \(G_{y'}\) is of order prime to the characteristic of \(k\) [3 VI].
5. The Weil formula (compare [3 VI § 4]).
All the schemes considered in this section will be defined over an algebraically closed base field \(k\).
We shall use the following notations:
\[\Lambda_\nu=\mathbb Z/\ell^\nu\mathbb Z,\quad \ell\text{ being a given prime number, distinct from the characteristic of }k.\]
Let \(\mathbb Z_\ell\longrightarrow\Lambda_\nu\) be the canonical homomorphism. It induces a homomorphism
\[\lambda_\nu:\mathbb Z_\ell[G]\longrightarrow\Lambda_\nu[G]\]
and consequently [VIII 9] a reduction homomorphism:
\[\lambda_\nu^*:K^\bullet(\mathbb Z_\ell[G])\longrightarrow K^\bullet(\Lambda_\nu[G]).\]

The images under \(\lambda_ u^*\) of the elements associated with the modules \(\operatorname{Sw}_y\), \(\operatorname{Sw}'_y\) will be denoted by \(\widehat{\operatorname{Sw}}_y^{\Lambda_ u}\), \(\widehat{\operatorname{Sw}}_y'{}^{\Lambda_ u}\).
The reduction homomorphism \(\lambda_ u^*\) is in fact an isomorphism [IX 1.5]. The inverse of \(\lambda_ u^*\) will be denoted by \(\mu_ u\).
The constant sheaf on the scheme \(X\) associated to the ring \(\Lambda_\nu\) will be denoted by \(\Lambda_{\nu,X}\).
Let \(V\) be an open subset of \(X\), \(j:V\lhook\joinrel\longrightarrow X\) the canonical immersion. We set
\[j_!(\Lambda_{\nu,V})=\Lambda_{\nu,V,X}.\]

blocks.title: Proposition 5.1.
blocks.html:
Proposition 5.1. — Let \(C'\) be an algebraic curve, smooth and proper over the algebraically closed field \(k\), \(G\) a finite group acting on \(C'\), \(C=C'/G\), \(p:C'\longrightarrow C\) the canonical projection, \(U\) a dense open subset of \(C\) such that \(U'=p^{-1}(U)\) is a principal covering of \(U\) with group \(G\), \(Y=C-U\), \(Y'=p^{-1}(Y)=C'-U'\), and let \(\operatorname{EP}(C)\) be the Euler-Poincaré characteristic of \(C\) (equal to \(2-2g\) if \(C\) is connected of genus \(g\)).

Under these conditions we have the formula of Weil:

\[(5.2)\qquad \operatorname{cl}_{K^ ullet(\Lambda_ u[G])} igl(R\Gamma_{C'}(\Lambda_{ u,U',C'}) igr)=\operatorname{EP}(C)\operatorname{cl}_{K^ ullet(\Lambda_ u[G])}(\Lambda_ u[G])-\sum_{y\in Y}\widehat{\operatorname{Sw}}'_y{}^{\Lambda_ u}\]
where the left-hand side of (6.2) is defined thanks to 2.1.

Proof. Denote by \(S_1\) (resp. \(S_2\)) the first (resp. the second) member of the formula to be proved. Since the reduction homomorphism \(\lambda_\nu^*\) is in fact an isomorphism, it suffices to prove that
On the other hand (3.3.1) the trace homomorphism:
\[K^\bullet(\mathbb Z_\ell[G])\xrightarrow{\ \operatorname{tr}\ }\mathcal F(G,\mathbb Z_\ell)\]
is a monomorphism. Consequently it suffices to prove that
\[\operatorname{tr}(\mu_\nu(S_1))=\operatorname{tr}(\mu_\nu(S_2)).\]
The character \(\sigma_2=\operatorname{tr}(\mu_\nu(S_2))\) is evidently given by the formula
\[(5.2.1)\qquad \sigma_2=\operatorname{EP}(C)r_G-\sum_{y\in Y}\sigma'_y,\]
where \(\sigma'_y\) is the character of \(\operatorname{Sw}'_y\) ((4.2.7) and (4.2.3)), \(r_G\) the regular representation.
Let us now consider the reduction homomorphisms for the traces:
\[\mathcal F(G,\mathbb Z_\ell)\xrightarrow{\ \lambda_n^\natural\ }\mathcal F(G,\mathbb Z/\ell^n\mathbb Z).\]
We evidently have
\[\mathcal F(G,\mathbb Z_\ell)=\varprojlim_n\mathcal F(G,\mathbb Z/\ell^n\mathbb Z).\]
It therefore suffices to prove that we have, for any \(n\)
\[\lambda_n^ atural igl(\operatorname{tr}(\mu_ u(S_1)) igr)=\lambda_n^ atural(\sigma_2).\]
The left-hand side is equal to \(\operatorname{tr}(\lambda_n^*(\mu_\nu(S_1)))\), now
\[\lambda_n^*(\mu_\nu(S_1))=\operatorname{cl}_{K^\bullet(\Lambda_n[G])}\bigl(R\Gamma_{C'}(\Lambda_{\nu,U',C'})\bigr),\]
thanks to the Künneth formula (SGA 4 XVIII)
\[R\Gamma_{C'}(\Lambda_{n,U',C'})\mathbin{\overset L\otimes}_{\Lambda_n}\Lambda_ u\xrightarrow{\sim}R\Gamma_{C'}(\Lambda_{ u,U',C'})\qquad ext{for }n\geq u.\]
We are thus reduced to proving the equality:
\[\operatorname{tr}\!\left(\operatorname{cl}_{K^\bullet(\Lambda_n[G])}\bigl(R\Gamma_{C'}(\Lambda_{n,U',C'})\bigr)\right)=\lambda_n^\natural(\sigma_2).\]
Denote the left-hand side of this equality by \(\pi\) and let \(g\in G\). The element \(g\) evidently induces an endomorphism of the distinguished triangle:
\[ \xymatrix{ &R\Gamma_{Y'}(\Lambda_{n,Y'})\ar[dl]\ar[dr]&\\ R\Gamma_{C'}(\Lambda_{n,U',C'})\ar[rr]&&R\Gamma_{C'}(\Lambda_{n,C'}) } \]
We therefore have:
\[ \tau(g)=\operatorname{Tr}^*gR\Gamma_{C'}(\Lambda_{n,U',C'}) =\operatorname{Tr}^*gR\Gamma_{C'}(\Lambda_{n,C'}) -\operatorname{Tr}^*gR\Gamma_{Y'}(\Lambda_{n,Y'}). \]

(For brevity we have written \(\operatorname{Tr}^*\) instead of \(\operatorname{Tr}_{\Lambda_n}^*\)). Now we have, applying a trivial "Lefschetz formula" on the discrete space \(Y'\) on which \(g\) acts:
\[ \operatorname{Tr}^*gR\Gamma_{Y'}(\Lambda_{n,Y'}) =\text{number of fixed points of the restriction }g_{Y'}\text{ of }g\text{ to }Y' =\sum_{\substack{y'\in Y'\\gy'=y'}}1. \]
On the other hand the Lefschetz formula (III 4.7) for \(g\) acting on \(C'\) gives us, if \(g\ne e\):
\[ \operatorname{Tr}^*gR\Gamma_{C'}(\Lambda_{n,C'}) =\sum_{y'\in Y'}v_{y'}(g) =\text{the sum of the multiplicities of the fixed points of the map }g_{C'}:C'\longrightarrow C', \]
and we have:
\[ \operatorname{Tr}^*gR\Gamma_{C'}(\Lambda_{n,C'}) =\operatorname{EP}(C')\cdot1_{\Lambda}\quad\text{for }g=e, \]
thanks to the explicit computation of the \(H^i(C',\Lambda_{n,C'})\), via (SGA 4 IX 4.7) and
Künneth (SGA 4 XVII).
From this we therefore deduce the following formula for \(\tau\):

\[ (5.2.2)\qquad \tau(g)= \begin{cases} \displaystyle\sum_{\substack{y'\in Y'\\g(y')=y'}}(v_{y'}(g)-1)&\text{if }g\ne e,\\[8pt] \operatorname{EP}(C')-\operatorname{card}(Y')&\text{if }g=e. \end{cases} \]
Comparing with (5.2.1) we easily find \(\tau=\lambda_n^\natural(\sigma_2)\).
Indeed, if \(g\ne e\) the equality between \(\tau(g)\) and \(\lambda_n^\natural(\sigma_2)\) is immediate (4.5.2).
If \(g=e\) the formula to be verified reads:
\[ \operatorname{EP}(C')-\operatorname{card}(Y') =N\operatorname{EP}(C)-\sum_{y\in Y}\sigma'_y(e), \quad\text{where }N=\operatorname{card}(G), \]
that is to say (4.5.2):
\[ \operatorname{EP}(C')-N\operatorname{EP}(C) =-\deg(\mathfrak D_{C'/C}), \]
which is nothing other than the classical Hurwitz formula.
6. Definition of the local terms \(\varepsilon_x^\Delta(F)\)

Let \(C\) be a connected smooth algebraic curve over the algebraically closed field \(k\), \(A\) a \(\Lambda\)-algebra (\(\Lambda=\mathbb Z/\ell^\nu\mathbb Z\), \(\ell\) distinct from the characteristic of \(k\)) and \(\Delta\subset D^+(A)\) a triangulated subcategory of \(D^+(A)\) stable under direct factors. Let \(F\) be a complex of sheaves of \(A\)-modules on \(C\) such that the following conditions are satisfied:
i) For every geometric point \(\bar x\in C(\bar k)\), we have \(F_{\bar x}\in(\Delta)\).

ii) There exists a non-empty open subset of \(C\) on which \(F\) is "locally constant", or what amounts to the same thing, there exist a non-empty open subset \(U\) of \(C\) and a connected Galois covering \(C'\xrightarrow p C\) of \(C\), with group \(G\), such that, if \(F'=p^*(F)\) is the inverse image of \(F\) by \(p\), then \(F'|U'\) is constant (\(U'=p^{-1}(U)\)).

The sheaf \(F|U\) is therefore defined by a complex \(M\) of \(A\)-modules on which \(G\) acts. If we denote by \(\eta\) (resp. \(\eta'\)) the generic point of \(C\) (resp. \(C'\)) and by \(\bar\eta\) (resp. \(\bar\eta'\)) a geometric point over \(\eta\) (resp. \(\eta'\)) we have:
\[ p(\eta')=\eta, \] \[ (6.1.1)\qquad F_{\bar\eta}\simeq F'_{\bar\eta'}=M. \]

As a complex of \(A\)-modules \(M\) is isomorphic to \(F_{\bar\eta}\), and thanks to condition i) it is an object of \(\Delta\). Therefore (3.5) for every \(x\in C(\bar k)\), \(\operatorname{Hom}_{\Lambda[G]}^\bullet(\widehat{\operatorname{Sw}}_x^\Lambda,M)\) is an object of \(\Delta\), whence an element of \(K(\Delta)\).

In fact the object of \(\Delta\) defined by \(\operatorname{Hom}_{\Lambda[G]}^\bullet(\widehat{\operatorname{Sw}}_x^\Lambda,M)\) does not depend, up to isomorphism, on the Galois covering \(C'\xrightarrow p C\) which trivializes \(F|U\). To prove this it suffices to compare the elements associated to two coverings \(C'\xrightarrow p C\), \(C''\xrightarrow{p'}C'\xrightarrow p C\). In this case, if \(G'\) is the Galois group of the covering \(C''\xrightarrow{p''}C\), then \(G\) is a quotient group of \(G'\). Let
\[ \varphi:\mathbb Z_\ell[G']\longrightarrow\mathbb Z_\ell[G] \]
be the associated canonical epimorphism. The Swan modules are then related by the relation
\[ \operatorname{Sw}_{G,x}\simeq \operatorname{Sw}_{G',x}\otimes_{\mathbb Z_\ell[G']}\mathbb Z_\ell[G], \]
which follows immediately from the fact that the character \(a_{G,x}\) is the character induced from \(G'\) to \(G\) ([3] VI Prop. 3).
On the other hand we have an isomorphism of complexes of \(A[G']\)-modules:
\[ F_{\bar\eta'}\simeq[F_{\bar\eta''}]_{(\varphi)}. \]

From this we deduce a canonical isomorphism of complexes of \(A\)-modules between \(\operatorname{Hom}_{\Lambda[G]}^\bullet(\widehat{\operatorname{Sw}}_{G,x}^\Lambda,F_{\bar\eta'})\) and \(\operatorname{Hom}_{\Lambda[G']}^\bullet(\widehat{\operatorname{Sw}}_{G',x}^\Lambda,F_{\bar\eta''})\) (adjunction formula).
We shall use the following notations:

\[ (6.2.1)\qquad \alpha_x^\Delta(F)=\operatorname{cl}_{K(\Delta)}\!\left(\operatorname{Hom}_{\Lambda[G]}^\bullet(\widehat{\operatorname{Sw}}_x^\Lambda,F_{\bar\eta})\right) \] \[ (6.2.2)\qquad \varepsilon_x^\Delta(F)=\alpha_x^\Delta(F)+\left(\operatorname{cl}_{K(\Delta)}(F_{\bar\eta})-\operatorname{cl}_{K(\Delta)}(F_{\bar x})\right). \]

Of course in formula (6.2.1) \(F_{\bar\eta}\) is endowed with the structure of \(A[G]\)-module thanks to the isomorphism (6.1.1). Note that if \(x\in C(\bar k)\) is such that \(C'\) is tamely ramified at \(x\), we shall have (4.6) \(\operatorname{Sw}_x=0\) hence \(\alpha_x(\Delta)=0\), so in this case we shall have (in accordance with Ogg-Shafarevich):
\[ (6.2.3)\qquad \varepsilon_x^\Delta(F)=\operatorname{cl}_{K(\Delta)}(F_{\bar\eta})-\operatorname{cl}_{K(\Delta)}(F_{\bar x}). \]
It is clear that \(\varepsilon_x^\Delta(F)=0\) if \(x\notin Y=C-U\), and consequently there exist only finitely many \(x\) such that \(\varepsilon_x^\Delta(F)\ne0\).
Let us now give examples of categories \(\Delta\) and of complexes of sheaves \(F\) such that the conditions stated above are satisfied.

a) Let \(\Delta=D^b(A)_{\mathrm{coh}}\) (VIII 5) and let \(F\) be a complex of sheaves of \(A\)-modules which is constructible (SGA 4 XVII) and with bounded cohomology. In this case \(\varepsilon_x^\Delta(F)\in K_\bullet(A)\).

b) We take \(\Delta=D(A)_{\mathrm{parf}}\) (VIII 6) and \(F\) a complex of sheaves of \(A\)-modules which is constructible and of finite tor-dimension. In this case \(\varepsilon_x^\Delta(F)\in K^\bullet(A)\).

c) Let \(A\), \(B\) be two \(\Lambda\)-algebras and \(\varphi:A\longrightarrow B\) a homomorphism of \(\Lambda\)-algebras. We take \(\Delta=D^b(B\operatorname{ rel }A)_{\mathrm{coh}}\) (VIII 10) and \(F\) a complex of sheaves of \(B\)-modules, constructible as a sheaf of complexes of \(A\)-modules and with bounded cohomology. We obtain \(\varepsilon_x^\Delta(F)\in K_\bullet(B\operatorname{ rel }A)\). Of course we can vary as above to obtain, for example, elements \(\varepsilon_x^\Delta(F)\in K^\bullet(B\operatorname{ rel }A)\).
7. Euler-Poincaré formula.

blocks.title: Theorem 7.1.
blocks.html:
Theorem 7.1.

Let \(C\) be a connected algebraic curve, smooth and proper over the algebraically closed field \(k\), \(\eta\) the generic point of \(C\), \(\Delta\subset D^+(A)\) a triangulated subcategory of \(D^+(A)\) stable under direct factors, \(F\) a complex of sheaves of \(A\)-modules such that conditions i), ii) of 7.1 are satisfied. Under these conditions we have \(R\Gamma_C(F)\in\operatorname{Ob}(\Delta)\), and the following formula (Euler-Poincaré formula) holds:
\[ (7.2)\qquad \operatorname{cl}_{K(\Delta)}(R\Gamma_C(F))=\operatorname{EP}(C)\operatorname{cl}_{K(\Delta)}(F_{\bar\eta})-\sum_{x\in C(k)}\varepsilon_x^\Delta(F), \]
where the \(\varepsilon_x^\Delta(F)\) are defined in 6.2 (formula 6.2.2).
The proof of this theorem will be carried out in several steps.
a) Let \(\Psi\) be a complex, bounded below, of sheaves of \(\Lambda[G]\)-modules on \(C\). We have an isomorphism:
\[ (7.3)\qquad R\Gamma^G(R\Gamma_C(\Psi))\simeq R\Gamma_C(R\underline\Gamma^G(\Psi)), \]

where \(\underline\Gamma^G\) is the functor: "subsheaf of \(G\)-invariant sections". Indeed \(\Gamma_C\) transforms an injective sheaf of \(\Lambda[G]\)-modules into an injective \(\Lambda[G]\)-module and \(\underline\Gamma^G\) transforms an injective sheaf of \(\Lambda[G]\)-modules into an injective sheaf of \(\Lambda\)-modules (use for this, for example, obvious adjoints of \(\Gamma_C\) and \(\underline\Gamma^G\)), and on the other hand we have
\[\Gamma^G\circ\Gamma_C=\Gamma_C\circ\underline\Gamma^G.\]
Taking the derived functors of both sides and applying ([5] prop. 3.1), we thus obtain (7.3).
b) Let \(\Phi\) be a complex, bounded below, of sheaves of \(\Lambda\)-modules on \(C\), zero outside \(U\). We have an isomorphism:
\[ (7.4)\qquad \Phi\simeq R\underline\Gamma^G(p_*(\Phi')),\quad\text{where }\Phi'=p^*(\Phi). \]

Let us first remark that the functors \(p^*\), \(\underline\Gamma^G\circ p_*\) establish quasi-inverse equivalences between the category of sheaves of \(\Lambda\)-modules on \(C'\) on which \(G\) acts and which are zero outside \(U'=p^{-1}(U)\), and the category of sheaves of \(\Lambda\)-modules on \(C\) which are zero outside \(U\). We shall therefore have the isomorphism:
\[\Phi\xrightarrow{\sim}R((\underline\Gamma^G\circ p_*)\circ p^*)(\Phi).\]
On the other hand we can again apply ([5] Prop 3.1), which gives us, taking into account that \(Rp^*=p^*\), \(Rp_*=p_*\):
\[ \Phi\xrightarrow{\sim}R(\underline\Gamma^G\circ p_*)(p^*(\Phi))\simeq(R\underline\Gamma^G)(p_*p^*(\Phi)), \]
which is the desired isomorphism.
c) Let \(\Phi\) be a complex of sheaves as in b). We have the isomorphism
\[ (7.5)\qquad R\Gamma_C(\Phi)\simeq(R\Gamma^G)(R\Gamma_{C'}(\Phi')),\quad\text{where }\Phi'=p^*(\Phi). \]
Indeed we have:
\[ (R\Gamma^G)(R\Gamma_{C'}(\Phi'))\simeq(R\Gamma^G)(R\Gamma_C(p_*(\Phi')))\simeq R\Gamma_C(R\underline\Gamma^G(p_*(\Phi'))) \]
thanks to (7.3), and (7.5) follows from this thanks to the isomorphism (7.4).
d) Denote by \(i':U'\longrightarrow C'\), \(i:U\longrightarrow C\) the canonical inclusions and set:

To compute \(R\Gamma_C(\Phi)\) we may apply formula (7.5). On the other hand, with the notations of 7.1, \(\Phi'\simeq\Lambda_{U',C'}\mathbin{\overset L\otimes}_\Lambda M\) and the Kunneth formula (SGA 4 XVII) give us
\[ (7.6)\qquad R\Gamma_{C'}(\Phi')\simeq R\Gamma_{C'}(\Lambda_{U',C'}\mathbin{\overset L\otimes}_\Lambda M)\simeq R\Gamma_{C'}(\Lambda_{U',C'})\mathbin{\overset L\otimes}_\Lambda M. \]

This last isomorphism is in fact an isomorphism between the objects of \(D(\Lambda[G])\) defined by the two sides. Combining (7.6), (7.5) and formula (3.9.1), which is applicable thanks to 2.1, we obtain:

\[ (7.7)\qquad R\Gamma_C(F_{U,C})\simeq R\Gamma^G\!\left(R\Gamma_{C'}(\Lambda_{U',C'})\mathbin{\overset L\otimes}_\Lambda M\right)\simeq R\operatorname{Hom}_{\Lambda[G]}\!\left(R\Gamma_{C'}(\Lambda_{U',C'})^\vee,M\right). \]
Hence, taking into account \(M\simeq F_{\bar\eta}\in\operatorname{Ob}(\Delta)\) (condition (i)) and (3.6), we find:
\[R\Gamma_C(F_{U,C})\in\operatorname{Ob}(\Delta).\]
On the other hand we have the distinguished triangle (where \(Y=C-U\)):
\[ (7.8)\qquad\xymatrix{&R\Gamma_Y(F|Y)\ar[dl]&\\R\Gamma_C(F_{U,C})\ar[rr]&&R\Gamma_C(F)\ar[ul]} \]

where evidently \(R\Gamma_Y(F)=\bigoplus_{y\in Y}F_{\bar y}\in\operatorname{Ob}(\Delta)\), since \(F_{\bar y}\in\operatorname{Ob}(\Delta)\) (condition (i)). It follows therefore that \(R\Gamma_C(F)\in\operatorname{Ob}(\Delta)\), that is, the first assertion of the theorem. The distinguished triangle (7.8) gives us moreover the relation:
\[ \operatorname{cl}_{K(\Delta)}(R\Gamma_C(F))=\operatorname{cl}_{K(\Delta)}(R\Gamma_C(F_{U,C}))+\sum_{y\in Y}\operatorname{cl}_{K(\Delta)}(F_{\bar y}), \]
and using the isomorphism 7.7:

\[ \operatorname{cl}_{K(\Delta)}(R\Gamma_C(F))=\operatorname{cl}_{K(\Delta)}\!\left(R\operatorname{Hom}_{\Lambda[G]}(R\Gamma_{C'}(\Lambda_{U',C'})^\vee,F_{\bar\eta})\right)+\sum_{y\in Y}\operatorname{cl}_{K(\Delta)}(F_{\bar y}). \]
To obtain the Euler-Poincaré formula it now suffices to use the Weil formula (5.2). Indeed, first 4.4, (5.2) and 3.6 (in the particular case \(B=\Lambda[G]\)) give us

\[ \operatorname{cl}_{K^\bullet(\Lambda[G])}(R\Gamma_{C'}(\Lambda_{U',C'}))=\operatorname{cl}_{K^\bullet(\Lambda[G])}(R\Gamma_{C'}(\Lambda_{U',C'})^\vee). \] \[ \begin{aligned} \operatorname{cl}_{K(\Delta)}(R\Gamma_C(F)) ={}&\operatorname{hom}\!\left(\operatorname{cl}_{K^\bullet(\Lambda[G])}(R\Gamma_{C'}(\Lambda_{U',C'})),\operatorname{cl}_{K(D(\Lambda[G])_\Delta)}(F_{\bar\eta})\right)+\sum_{y\in Y}\operatorname{cl}_{K(\Delta)}(F_{\bar y})\\ ={}&\sum_{y\in Y}\operatorname{cl}_{K(\Delta)}(F_{\bar y})+\operatorname{hom}\!\left(\operatorname{EP}(C)\operatorname{cl}_{K^\bullet(\Lambda[G])}(\Lambda[G])-\sum_{y\in Y}\left(\widehat{\operatorname{Sw}}_y^\Lambda+\operatorname{cl}_{K^\bullet(\Lambda[G])}(\Lambda[G])\right),\operatorname{cl}_{K(D(\Lambda[G])_\Delta)}(F_{\bar\eta})\right)\\ ={}&\sum_{y\in Y}\operatorname{cl}_{K(\Delta)}(F_{\bar y})+\operatorname{EP}(C)\operatorname{cl}_{K(\Delta)}(F_{\bar\eta})-\sum_{y\in Y}\left(\alpha_y^\Delta(F)+\operatorname{cl}_{K(\Delta)}(F_{\bar\eta})\right)\\ ={}&\operatorname{EP}(C)\operatorname{cl}_{K(\Delta)}(F_{\bar\eta})-\sum_{y\in Y}\varepsilon_y^\Delta(F). \end{aligned} \]
Taking into account that \(\varepsilon_x^\Delta(F)=0\) for \(x\in U\), this is nothing other than formula (7.2).
For a complex of sheaves \(F\) satisfying the conditions of 7.1, we shall introduce the notation
\[\operatorname{EP}_\Delta(C,F)=\operatorname{cl}_{K(\Delta)}(R\Gamma_C(F)).\]

blocks.title: Corollary 7.9.
blocks.html:
Corollary 7.9.

If \(F\), \(F'\) are complexes of sheaves satisfying the conditions of 7.1 and if they are locally isomorphic (for the étale topology) as complexes of \(A\)-modules, we have:

\[(7.10)\qquad \operatorname{EP}_\Delta(C,F)=\operatorname{EP}_\Delta(C,F').\]

In particular, if \(F\) is locally constant with value \(M\), we have:

\[(7.11)\qquad \operatorname{EP}_\Delta(C,F)=\operatorname{EP}(C)\operatorname{cl}_{K(\Delta)}(M).\]

blocks.title: Corollary 7.12.
blocks.html:
Corollary 7.12.

Let \(C\) be a connected smooth algebraic curve, proper over \(k\), \(V\) a non-empty open subset of \(C\), \(\Delta\) a subcategory of \(D^+(A)\) as in 7.1, and \(F\) a complex of sheaves of \(A\)-modules on \(V\) such that its restriction to a suitable non-empty open subset of \(V\) is trivialized by a Galois covering of \(V\). Suppose moreover that \(F_{\bar x}\in\operatorname{Ob}(\Delta)\) for every \(x\in V\). Under these conditions we have:

\[(*)\qquad R\Gamma_!(V,F),\ R\Gamma(V,F)\in\operatorname{Ob}(\Delta),\] \[(**)\qquad \operatorname{cl}_{K(\Delta)}(R\Gamma_!(V,F))=\operatorname{cl}_{K(\Delta)}(R\Gamma(V,F)).\]

Set \(\operatorname{EP}_\Delta(V,F)=\operatorname{cl}_{K(\Delta)}(R\Gamma_V(F))\) and

\[ \operatorname{EP}(V)=\operatorname{EP}_{D(\mathbb F_\ell)_{\mathrm{coh}}}(V,\mathbb F_\ell)=\operatorname{EP}(C)-\operatorname{card}(Z), \]

where \(Z=C-V\) and where \(\mathbb F_\ell\) is the sheaf on \(C\) defined in dimension zero by the constant sheaf associated to \(\mathbb F_\ell=\mathbb Z/\ell\mathbb Z\). We then have the formula:
\[ (7.13)\qquad \operatorname{EP}_\Delta(V,F)=\operatorname{EP}(V)\operatorname{cl}_{K(\Delta)}(F_{\bar\eta})-\sum_{x\in V(k)}\varepsilon_x^\Delta(F)-\sum_{x\in Z}\alpha_x^\Delta(F), \]
where \(\varepsilon_x^\Delta\) and \(\alpha_x^\Delta\) are defined by (6.2.1), (6.2.2).
We shall use the following lemma:

blocks.title: Lemma 7.14.
blocks.html:
Lemma 7.14.

Let \(K\) be the field of fractions of a strictly local discrete valuation ring \(R\), \(W=\operatorname{Spec}K\), \(A\) a noetherian ring which is annihilated by an integer \(n\) prime to the residue characteristic \(p\) of \(R\), and \(F\) a complex of sheaves of \(A\)-modules on \(W\) trivialized by a finite extension of \(K\). If \(\Delta\) is a triangulated subcategory of \(D^+(A)\) stable under direct factors and if \(F_{\bar\eta}\in\operatorname{Ob}(\Delta)\) (\(\bar\eta\) the generic point of \(S\)) then \(R\Gamma_W(F)\in\operatorname{Ob}(\Delta)\) and \(\operatorname{cl}_{K(\Delta)}(R\Gamma_W(F))=0\).

Proof of 7.14. Let \(\pi\) be the fundamental group of \(W\). Then \(F\) corresponds to a complex of \(A\)-modules \(M^\bullet\) with group \(\pi\) of operators. The complex of \(A\)-modules underlying \(M^\bullet\) is isomorphic to \(F_{\bar\eta}\) and consequently it belongs to \(\operatorname{Ob}(\Delta)\). Let us introduce the quotient group \(\bar\pi\) of \(\pi\) by its unique \(p\)-Sylow subgroup \(P\) [4, II 33], that is, the projective limit of the discrete quotient groups of \(\pi\) which are of order prime to \(p\), where \(p\) is the characteristic exponent of the residue field of \(R\). (If \(p=1\), we set \(\bar\pi=\pi\).) We thus have the exact sequence
\[1\longrightarrow P\longrightarrow\pi\longrightarrow\bar\pi\longrightarrow0,\]
and we obtain
\[R\Gamma_W(F)\simeq R\Gamma^\pi(M^\bullet)\simeq R\Gamma^{\bar\pi}(R\Gamma^P(M^\bullet))\]

("Hochschild-Serre isomorphism"). Now \(P\) acts on \(M^\bullet\) via a finite quotient group, which is therefore a \(p\)-group, hence of order prime to \(n\). Therefore we have \(R\Gamma^P(M^\bullet)\simeq\Gamma^P(M^\bullet)\), and \(\Gamma^P(M^\bullet)\) is a direct factor of \(M^\bullet\), and therefore belongs to \(\operatorname{Ob}(\Delta)\).

On the other hand \(\bar\pi=\prod_{\ell\ne p}\mathbb Z_\ell\) ([4], II 33). We are thus reduced to proving that if \(M^\bullet\in\operatorname{Ob}(\Delta)\), then \(R\Gamma^{\bar\pi}(M^\bullet)\in\operatorname{Ob}(\Delta)\) and \(\operatorname{cl}_{K(\Delta)}(R\Gamma^{\bar\pi}(M^\bullet))=0\). For this let \(f\) be a topological generator of \(\bar\pi\). We may suppose that the components \(M^i\) of \(M\) are \(\bar\pi\)-modules such that \(H^j(\bar\pi,M^i)=0\) if \(j\geq1\). There exists then a distinguished triangle:
\[ (7.15)\qquad \xymatrix{M^\bullet\ar[dr]^{1-f}&R\Gamma^{\bar\pi}(M^\bullet)\ar[l]\ar[d]\\&M^\bullet.} \]
Indeed, on the one hand it is clear that \(R\Gamma^{\bar\pi}=\operatorname{Ker}(1-f)\). On the other hand \(1-f:M^\bullet\longrightarrow M^\bullet\) is surjective. Indeed
\[H^1(\bar\pi,M)=M^\bullet/(1-f)M^\bullet,\]

(see for this computation [4] pp. 197) in a substantially analogous case), whence \(M^\bullet=(1-f)M^\bullet\). The triangle (7.15) gives us at the same time \(R\Gamma^{\bar\pi}(M^\bullet)\in\operatorname{Ob}(\Delta)\) and \(\operatorname{cl}_{K(\Delta)}(R\Gamma^{\bar\pi}(M^\bullet))=0\), which proves 7.14.

Proof of 7.12. The fact that \(R\Gamma_!V(F)\in\operatorname{Ob}(\Delta)\) follows immediately from 7.1 applied to the complex \(i_!(F)\), where \(i:V\longrightarrow C\); indeed we have \(R\Gamma_!V(F)=R\Gamma_C(i_!F)\). To prove that \(R\Gamma_V(F)\in\operatorname{Ob}(\Delta)\), we consider the distinguished triangle:
\[ \xymatrix{&Q^\bullet\ar[dl]\\Ri_!(F)\ar[rr]&&Ri_*(F)\ar[ul]}, \]
where \(Q^\bullet\) is concentrated on \(Z=C-V\), whence, applying \(R\Gamma_C\), the distinguished triangle:
\[ (7.16)\qquad \xymatrix{&R\Gamma_Z(Q^\bullet|Z)\ar[dl]\\R\Gamma_!V(F)\ar[rr]&&R\Gamma_V(F)\ar[ul]}. \]

Since \(R\Gamma_!V(F)\in\operatorname{Ob}(\Delta)\), it follows that \(R\Gamma_V(F)\in\operatorname{Ob}(\Delta)\) if and only if \(R\Gamma_Z(Q^\bullet|Z)\in\operatorname{Ob}(\Delta)\), that is, \((Ri_*(F))_x\in\operatorname{Ob}(\Delta)\) for every \(x\in Z\). Recall that to compute \((Ri_*(F))_x\) one considers the cartesian square
\[ \xymatrix{\widetilde V\ar[r]^j\ar[d]_u&C_x\ar[d]\\V\ar[r]&C} \]
where \(C_x\) is the strict localization corresponding to the geometric point \(x\); we have \((Ri_*(F))_x\simeq R\Gamma_{\widetilde V}(u^*F)\), by virtue of 7.14, and we have

hence \((Ri_*(F))_x\in\operatorname{Ob}(\Delta)\) and \(\operatorname{cl}_{K(\Delta)}(Ri_*(F))_x=0\). It follows that \(\operatorname{cl}_{K(\Delta)}(R\Gamma_Z(Q^\bullet|Z))=0\) and the distinguished triangle (7.16) gives us
\[ \operatorname{cl}_{K(\Delta)}(R\Gamma_!V(F))=\operatorname{cl}_{K(\Delta)}(R\Gamma_V(F)). \]
Finally, to establish (7.13) it suffices to use the Euler-Poincaré formula (7.2) for the complex of sheaves \(i_!(F)\):

\[ \operatorname{EP}_\Delta(V,F)=\operatorname{cl}_{K(\Delta)}(R\Gamma_V(F))=\operatorname{cl}_{K(\Delta)}(R\Gamma_C(i_!F))=\operatorname{EP}(C)\operatorname{cl}_{K(\Delta)}(F_{\bar\eta})-\sum_{x\in C(k)}\varepsilon_x^\Delta(i_!(F)). \]
Now we have:
\[ \sum_{x\in C(k)}\varepsilon_x^\Delta(i_!(F))=\sum_{x\in V(k)}\varepsilon_x^\Delta(F)+\sum_{x\in Z}\varepsilon_x^\Delta(F). \]
But for \(x\in Z\) we have \((i_!(F))_x=0\) and consequently for such a \(x\) we have:
\[ \varepsilon_x^\Delta(i_!(F))=\alpha_x^\Delta(F)+\operatorname{cl}_{K(\Delta)}(F_{\bar\eta}), \]
whence formula (7.13).
